content: We can frame naturally occurring biological agents (such as invasive pathogenic bacteria or spreading viral populations) as swarm of autonomous agents driven by an intrinsic curiosity module (ICM)-like internal reward mechanism, where exploration of new host niches generates an intrinsic 'curiosity reward' that drives their spread even in the absence of immediate extrinsic fitness gains. This perspective reinterprets unprovoked viral spillover into new host species as the biological equivalent of an ICM-powered UAV swarm exploring uncharted territory for target acquisition.
